name: fasolakia lathera
instructions: saute yellow onion in a generous amount of olive oil. add garlic and let it bloom briefly (don't brown). add green beans, potatoes, canned tomatoes, a pinch of sugar, salt, pepper, and water to barely cover. braise covered ~45 min until everything is very tender — green beans should be soft, not al dente (that's the whole point of lathera). finish with dill.
notes:
greek 'lathera' (oily) braise — olive oil is both medium and flavor, don't skimp. green beans are not supposed to be crunchy.

SUGAR AND DILL ARE THE DIFFERENTIATORS: on paper this dish reads as a close cousin of ratatouille (french vegetable braise with olive oil) and italian minestrone soup (tomato + vegetable + olive oil with beans). What pulls it firmly into greek territory is the combination of a pinch of sugar and dill. Sugar balances the canned-tomato acidity in a way the italian and french versions don't — the greek tomato base is rounder, less sharp. Dill is the herb signature — neither ratatouille (which leans on thyme and basil) nor minestrone (which prefers the soffrito-and-bean profile without fresh herbs) uses dill. Without either of these, the dish drifts toward one of those neighbors. Keep both.
